name: KEPIER 13

surface_od_ft.qualifier: absent (Surface Level ___ O.D. box left blank in header)

units: fathoms/feet/inches, transcribed verbatim — convert ONLY downstream. NB: this log is kept in FEET and INCHES; the Fathoms column is blank throughout, so ffi arrays are recorded [0, feet, inches].

annotations: depth-from-surface column left blank in original for this row; cumulative depth implied 0 ft 9 in (confirmed by next row's 3-0 depth = 0-9 + 2-3)

intervals.verbatim: Loamy Clay with Sand Partings. Water @ 9'-0".
annotations: water: 'Water @ 9'-0"' recorded in original lithology text
drift silt within Quaternary sequence, not bedrock siltstone
water: 'Water.' noted against this bed in original

intervals.verbatim: Grey Sandstone with Sandy Shale Bands. into
annotations: first bedrock; borehole bottomed here at 100 ft (30.48 m); the trailing word 'into' is left dangling in the original, implying the hole ended within (or at the top of) this sandstone

notes: Header: 'SECTION OF BOREHOLE AT KEPIER POWER STATION, DURHAM' for Messrs North-Eastern Electric Supply Co. Ltd.; communicated by / Borer: Wm. Coulson Ltd.; One-inch Map 27; Six-inch Map Durham; hole labelled 'No.13, Hole'; BGS Registration No. NZ 24 SE / 12-13, Page No. 1. DATE DISCREPANCY: the log header clearly reads 'Date of boring or sinking January, 1945', but the supplied BGS index metadata gives year 1939 — recorded year as written (1945); flag for reconciliation. Surface Level O.D. box left blank (no datum). A faint pencil note '? c103' sits beside the 'No.13, Hole' label; meaning unclear, not a seam name, recorded here only. Log is kept in feet & inches (Fathoms column blank throughout). Depth-from-surface omitted only for the top Soil row (implied 0-9). Clerk arithmetic checks out on every row (cumulative depth = running sum of thicknesses, e.g. 81-5 + 18-7 = 100-0); no sum slips found. No coal seams, seatearths, ironstones or pencil seam-correlation marginalia present — entirely drift over grey sandstone bedrock. Single page; final depth 100 ft 0 in = 30.48 m, matching the index depth exactly.
